name: Spread of Data — Range, Variance and Standard Deviation
whatIsIt: An average tells you the CENTRE of data, but not how SPREAD OUT it is. Measures of spread (dispersion) fix that. RANGE = highest − lowest. VARIANCE = the average of the squared distances from the mean. STANDARD DEVIATION (SD) = √variance — it says, on average, how far the values sit from the mean, in the original units.
whyNeeded: Two classes can have the same average mark but very different consistency. Spread measures tell you about reliability and risk — used in exam analysis, quality control, weather, and finance (a 'risky' investment has high SD).
realLifeUses: Comparing how consistent two students' marks are
Quality control: are the parts all close to the target size?
Weather: how much daily temperature varies
Finance: a high standard deviation means a riskier, more volatile return

vocabulary.meaning: The simplest spread: highest value − lowest value.
The average of the squared distances of values from the mean.
√variance — the typical distance of values from the mean, in original units.
How far one value is from the mean (value − mean).

story.title: Two Steady Batsmen
story.text: Two players both AVERAGE 5 runs, but one scores 4,5,5,6 and the other 0,2,8,10. 'Same average — are they the same?' Coach asks. Imran finds the spread. For 2,4,4,4,5,5,7,9 (mean 5) the squared deviations add to 32; variance = 32 ÷ 8 = 4, and SD = √4 = 2. 'A small SD means steady; a big SD means up-and-down,' Coach explains.
story.extractedProblem: A data set has mean 5 and squared deviations adding to 32 over 8 values. Find the variance and SD.
story.answerInStory: Variance 4 and SD 2 — variance = 32 ÷ 8 = 4, and SD = √4 = 2.

visual.caption: The mean is the balance point; spread measures how far the bars stray from it. Range = 9 − 2 = 7; this data's SD is 2.

examples.caption: Tightly packed data (4,5,6) has a small spread.
Widely scattered data (0,5,10) has a big spread — a larger SD.

standardMethod.summary: Range = max − min. For variance: find the mean, subtract it from each value (deviation), square each, average them. Standard deviation = √variance.
steps: Range: subtract the smallest value from the largest.
Find the mean of the data.
For each value, find (value − mean) and SQUARE it.
Variance = average of those squared deviations.

workedExamples.problem: A data set has variance 9. Find its standard deviation.

commonMistakes.mistake: Reporting the variance as the standard deviation (forgetting the square root).
commonMistakes.fix: SD = √variance. If the variance is 9, the SD is √9 = 3, not 9.

commonMistakes.mistake: Adding the largest and smallest instead of subtracting for the range.
commonMistakes.fix: Range = largest − smallest (a subtraction). For 2 and 9 it is 9 − 2 = 7.

easy.q: Standard deviation when the variance is 16?

medium.q: Squared deviations add to 32 over 8 values. Variance = 32 ÷ 8 = ?
If the variance is 4, the standard deviation is √4 = ?

challenge.q: A set has variance 25. Meena says the SD is 25. What's the fix?
hintLadder: SD is the square root of variance.

questions.q: Squared deviations sum to 50 over 2 values. Variance?

options.label: The square root of the variance

questions.answer: The square root of the variance

teachBackPrompt: Give a grown-up two sets of scores with the same average but different spread (like 4,5,5,6 vs 0,2,8,10). Show that range = max − min, and explain that SD = √variance measures the typical distance from the mean.

revisionCard.summary: Spread measures how scattered data is. Range = max − min. Variance = average of the SQUARED deviations from the mean. Standard deviation = √variance (back in original units). Small SD = consistent; large SD = scattered/risky. Don't forget the square root: SD, not variance.

rememberIt.unpack: Variance = average of (value - mean)^2. SD = sqrt(variance), back in the original units. Bigger SD = more spread out.

studentQuestions.q: Why square the differences for variance?
studentQuestions.a: So positives and negatives do not cancel, and big gaps count more. SD square-roots it back to normal units.

studentQuestions.q: What does a small SD mean?
studentQuestions.a: The data is tightly bunched near the mean; a large SD means it is widely spread out.

formulas.name: Standard deviation
formulas.remember: Square-root brings the units back to normal
